text: In this video, we present our technique on robotic left colectomy with complete mesocolic excision for splenic flexure cancer. A forty-nine-year-old male was presented with rectal bleeding and changes in bowel habits and underwent a colonoscopy which revealed ulcerative vegetative mass at the splenic flexure. For this operation, four eight millimeter robotic throw cards and one five millimeter assistant throw card for bedside surgeon were used.

After docking the robotic system, the surgeon incises the peritoneum and opens the aorta mesenteric dindo using monopolar scissors and bipolar forceps. The inferior mesenteric vein is exposed at the inferior border of the pancreas, ligated with Hem-o-lok clips, and transected. Dissection proceeds through embryological planes, preserving the left gonadal vein. The inferior mesenteric artery is demonstrated, and the left colic artery is isolated, ligated with clips, and transected at its origin while preserving the superior rectal artery. The transverse mesocolon is separated from the anterior surface of the pancreas, and the lesser sac is entered, continuing dissection to the level of the splenic flexure.

The surgeon continues medial to lateral dissection, carefully separating the left mesocolon from the Gerota's fascia and Toldt's fascia while preserving the left ureter, gonadal vessels, and autonomic nerves. The dissection progresses superiorly towards the spleen to achieve full mobilization of the splenic flexure.
The surgeon isolates the sigmoid artery, ligates it with clips, and transects it at its origin. Following this, the left colic vein is similarly ligated with clips and transected.

The surgeon dissects the left mesocolon, preparing it for transection, and exposes the distal dromones artery, which is ligated and transected. The connections between the superior and inferior mesenteric arteries are visible. Attention is turned to the middle colic vessels at the transverse mesocolon, where the left branch of the middle colic artery is dissected, ligated, and transected from its origin. The dissection of the splenic colic and gastrocolic ligaments facilitates the mobilization of the splenic flexure. The great omentum is fully mobilized and dissected at the lower border of the stomach, completing the mobilization of the splenic flexure.

The surgeon checks the perfusion of the distal colon using endocyanin green dye before transecting the left colon with a robotic stapler. Subsequently, the proximal colon is prepared and similarly checked for perfusion with ICG dye, followed by transection using the robotic stapler.

Traction sutures are placed for the anastomosis, followed by colotomy on both sides to position the stapler. A tension-free, side-to-side intracorporeal anastomosis is created using two robotic stapler fires. The anastomotic defect is closed in a two-layer fashion, with the inner layer closed using a running 3-0 V-Loc suture and the outer layer closed with interrupted single silk sutures.
The surgeon demonstrates the blood flow of the anastomosis using endocyanine green dye. The hemicolectomy specimen is visible with an intact mesocolic envelope and a ten-centimeter distal margin.
